coredata.dc:title: Encapsulation carbon QDs in In-MIL-68 to derive carbon QDs@In2S3 tube for photoreduction of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Meng
Wang, Chao
Wang, Lei
Xu, Jixiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon quantum dots@In2S3 hollow tube (CQDs@In2S3) with sheet-like hierarchical structure was synthesized by sulfidation CQDs@In-MIL-68. The CQDs@In2S3-2 exhibited the optimal Cr(VI) reductive ratio of nearly 100% after 45 ​min light irradiation in solution with pH of 4. The enhanced Cr(VI) reduction activity was ascribed to the efficient separation of electrons and holes after coupling of CQDs. The ·O2 − was the dominant active specie to reduce Cr(VI), and possible mechanism was proposed. This work provides thought for design of high-performance photocatalysts by loading electron-collecting species to remove environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
In2S3
Cr(VI) reduction
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52171140 , 51772162 , and 52072197 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2021ME022 ), Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2020ZD09 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of the prepared samples.
SEM images of (a) CQDs@In-MIL-68 and (b) CQDs@In2S3-2; (c, d) HRTEM images of CQDs@In2S3-2. (inset in c: TEM image of CQDs@In2S3-2)
XPS spectra of (a) survey, (b) In 3d, (c) S 2p, and (d) C 1s for the obtained In2S3 and CQDs@In2S3-2 samples.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra, (b) PL spectra, (c) transient photocurrent, and (d) EIS curves of the obtained samples.

(a) Cr(VI) reductive ratios in the presence of various catalysts and conditions; (b) the UV–visible absorption spectral changes of Cr(VI) solution; (c) effects of inorganic salts and (d) solution pH on the reduction of Cr(VI) over the CQDs@In2S3-2, (e) pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of Cr(VI) reduction under different conditions; (f) Cr(VI) reductive ratios over four cycles under CQDs@In2S3-2 catalysis. (Experimental conditions: catalyst dosage ​= ​0.17 ​g ​L−1; pH ​= ​5.5 except in (d); initial Cr(VI) concentration ​= ​10 ​mg ​L−1).
(a) Cr(VI) reductive ratios of CQDs@In2S3-2 in the presence of different radical scavengers, (b) mechanisms of Cr(VI) reduction under CQDs@In2S3-2 catalysis.

head.ce:title: Encapsulation carbon QDs in In-MIL-68 to derive carbon QDs@In2S3 tube for photoreduction of Cr(VI)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taishan Scholar Advantage and Characteristic Discipline Team of Eco-Chemical Process and Technology
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon quantum dots@In2S3 hollow tube (CQDs@In2S3) with sheet-like hierarchical structure was synthesized by sulfidation CQDs@In-MIL-68. The CQDs@In2S3-2 exhibited the optimal Cr(VI) reductive ratio of nearly 100% after 45 ​min light irradiation in solution with pH of 4. The enhanced Cr(VI) reduction activity was ascribed to the efficient separation of electrons and holes after coupling of CQDs. The ·O2 − was the dominant active specie to reduce Cr(VI), and possible mechanism was proposed. This work provides thought for design of high-performance photocatalysts by loading electron-collecting species to remove environmental pollutants.

In-MIL-68 was used as a matrix to encapsulate CQDs and as a precursor to derive hollow CQDs@In2S3 catalyst by sulfidation for efficient reduction of Cr (VI).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI)-containing wastewater from petroleum refining, electroplating, and leather processing industries can threat human health owing to the carcinogenic and mutagenic properties of Cr(VI) [1]. Reduction of Cr(VI) into Cr(III) via chemical and photocatalytic processes can relieve Cr(VI)-induced pollution [2]. In the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), a key point is to develop a catalyst that possesses high specific surface area for adsorption of Cr(VI), good light absorption and efficient charge carriers separation for providing more electrons to reduce Cr(VI). Among developed photocatalysts, metal sulfides have been used to reduce Cr(VI) owing to their broad light response and appropriate band structures [3–9]. Particularly, In2S3, an n-type semiconductor with band gap of about 2.6 ​eV, has attracted much attention. Nevertheless, pure In2S3 exhibits unsatisfactory photocatalytic performance due to the poor separation and low transfer kinetics of charge carriers. Thus, strategies of constructing In2S3-based type II/Z-scheme heterojunctions or coupling cocatalyst were used to accelerate the separation of electron-hole pairs, then to enhance the photocatalytic performance. For example, Lin et al. prepared Bi2S3@In2S3 Z-scheme photocatalyst to reduce Cr (VI) [10]. Dong et al. synthesized In2S3/MIL-53(Fe) type-II photocatalyst to reduce Cr (VI) [11], its apparent rate constant (k) value was nearly 4 times higher than that of In2S3. Coupling Ti3C2 with In2S3 could reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) completely within 6 ​min with a k value of 0.739 ​min−1 [12]. Other materials like ZnS [13], Bi2WO6 [14], BiVO4 [15], Fe2O3 [16], MoP [17], and multi-wall carbon nanotubes [18] also have been used to accelerate the separation of electrons and holes, pollutants degradation and H2 evolution of In2S3.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn attention due to their unique electron storage and transfer ability, as well as photoluminescence property. Studies about coupling CQDs with semiconductors to improve their photogenerated charge separation and catalytic activity have been reported. Li et al. prepared CQDs-modified BiOIO3, which exhibited high bisphenol degradation efficiency by increasing its charge transfer and light absorption [19]. Feng et al. employed CQDs as the electron mediators to regulate charge transfer between CdS and g-C3N4 for degradation of phenol and dyes [20]. Similarly, the catalytic performances of TiO2, ZnIn2S4, CuCo2O4, and MOFs were enhanced after coupling with CQDs [21–25]. In this regard, the photogenerated charge separation efficiency and Cr(VI) reductive performance of In2S3 may be improved after modification with CQDs.

Previous studies confirmed that hollow matrixes could reduce charge migration distance for assisting electrons and holes separation [26], could provide large specific surface area and abundant active sites for facilitating pollutants adsorption and redox reaction. Thus, integrating CQDs with hollow In2S3 may achieve efficient Cr(VI) reduction. Although In-MIL-68-derived In2S3 [27] and In2S3-based binary/ternary photocatalysts [4,10,16,28] have been designed to reduce Cr(VI), there are no reports about CQDs-modified hollow In2S3 tubes for reduction of Cr(VI).

In this work, rod-like In-MIL-68 was used as a matrix to encapsulate CQDs and as a precursor to derive hollow CQDs@In2S3 catalyst by sulfidation. The photocatalytic activity of CQDs@In2S3 in reduction of Cr(VI) was investigated.

All chemicals are analytical grade and used directly. Indium nitrate hydrate (In(NO3)3·xH2O), 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC), thiourea, and ethylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin. Methanol, ethanol, dimethyl formamide (DMF), potassium bromate (KBrO3), tert butyl alcohol (TBA), benzoquinone (BQ), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Citric acid was purchased from Tianjin Star Chemical Reagents Manufacturing Co., LTD. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

CQDs were prepared according to the previous work [29]. In-MIL-68 was synthesized by refluxing method [30]. For preparation of CQDs@In-MIL-68, 225 ​mg of In(NO3)3·xH2O and 275 ​mg of H2BDC were dissolved in 100 ​mL of DMF and stirred for 5 ​min. Then, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 ​mL of CQDs stock solution (the mass fraction of C is 0.32% obtained by elemental analysis) was added. The resulted solution was refluxed at 120 ​°C for 30 ​min. The obtained precipitate was centrifuged, washed with ethanol, and dried at 60 ​°C for 8 ​h.

50 ​mg of the prepared CQDs@In-MIL-68 powder and 150 ​mg of thiourea were dispersed in 7.5 ​mL of ethanol under stirring. The suspension was transferred into a 50 ​mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 ​°C for 24 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the yellow precipitate was centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol for three times, followed by vacuum-dried at 60 ​°C. The samples were denoted as CQDs@In2S3-x, where x is 1, 2, and 3, representing for CQDs solution volume, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 ​mL, respectively. In2S3 was also prepared via sulfidation In-MIL-68 with the same method.

5.0 ​mg of catalyst was dispersed in 30 ​mL of 10 ​mg ​L−1 Cr(VI) solution (K2Cr2O7, pH ​= ​5.5) and stirred in dark for 30 ​min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then the reaction system was irradiated by a 350 ​W Xenon lamp (λ ​≥ ​420 ​nm). At given time intervals, about 4 ​mL of the suspension was withdrawn and centrifuged. The concentration of Cr(VI) remained in the supernatant was determined by the spectrophotometric method using diphenylcarbazide (DPC) as the color reagent. The initial pH of the solution was adjusted using NaOH (0.1 ​M) and HCl (0.1 ​M). To evaluate the stability of the catalyst, the used catalyst was soaked in 1 ​mM HNO3 solution for 60 ​min to remove Cr(III), then washed with water and ethanol for eight times, dried before the next test.

The phase compositions of obtained samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement. The XRD pattern of prepared CQDs@In-MIL-68 matched with the simulated In-MIL-68 (Fig. S1). No CQDs-related signals were observed in XRD pattern of CQDs@In-MIL-68 owing to its weak diffraction peaks. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of In2S3 and CQDs@In2S3-x. The In2S3 exhibited three main diffraction peaks at 2θ ​= ​27.3°, 33.1°, and 47.8°, resulting from the reflections of the (311), (400) and (440) planes of the β-In2S3 (JCPDS No. 32–0456), respectively. After modification In2S3 with CQDs, the prepared CQDs@In2S3-x showed the analogous XRD patterns with that of In2S3, and the diffraction peaks from CQDs were not found. In addition, the intensities of the In2S3's peaks gradually increased with the increase of CQDs dose, indicating the increase of crystallinity of In2S3. Fig. 1b shows the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of CQDs, In2S3, CQDs@In2S3-2, and CQDs@In2S3-3 samples. For the CQDs, the C–H vibration at 2931 ​cm−1, CC bending vibration at 1396 ​cm−1, and CO stretching vibrations at 1690 and 1649 ​cm−1 were observed [29]. Compared to IR spectrum of In2S3, the peak at 1396 ​cm−1 of CQDs@In2S3-2 and CQDs@In2S3-3 became strong owing to overlapping with the CC vibration of CQDs (Fig. S2). This indicates the coupling of In2S3 and CQDs.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image showed that CQDs@In-MIL-68 had a rod-like morphology (Fig. 2 a). After sulfidation CQDs@In-MIL-68, the obtained CQDs@In2S3-2 exhibited hollow tubular structure constructed by nanosheets (Fig. 2b). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (inset in Fig. 2c) confirmed the hollow tubular structure of CQDs@In2S3-2. The CQDs showed sphere-like morphology with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 ​nm (Fig. S3), which was matched with the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [31]. In the high-resolution (HR)TEM image of CQDs@In2S3-2 (Fig. 2c), several CQDs attached on the edge of the nanosheets (red cycles) were found, further indicating that the CQDs have been successfully coupled with the In2S3. The lattice fringes of 0.27 and 0.21 ​nm were attributed to the (311) plane of In2S3 and (100) plane of CQDs, respectively (Fig. 2d). In addition, the specific surface area of CQDs@In2S3-2 was 69.6 ​m2 ​g−1 obtained by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S4).

The elemental compositions of In2S3 and CQDs@In2S3-2 were also analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum confirmed the presence of C (21.04%), In (24.19%), and S (37.75%) elements (Table S1) in CQDs@In2S3-2. The C content of CQDs@In2S3-2 was slightly higher than that value of In2S3 (19.56%) owing to the presence of CQDs. In the high-resolution In 3d spectrum of CQDs@In2S3-2 (Fig. 3 b), two peaks at 452.45 and 444.89 ​eV were observed, corresponding to In3+ 3d3/2 and In3+ 3d5/2, respectively. Compared to In 3d spectrum of In2S3, the In 3d peaks of the CQDs@In2S3-2 shifted to higher binding energy, implying the presence of electronic interactions and interfacial contacts between CQDs and In2S3 [29]. S 2p spectrum of CQDs@In2S3-2 (Fig. 3c) showed two peaks at 161.45 and 162.60 ​eV, attributing to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2−, respectively. The C 1s spectrum of CQDs@In2S3-2 was fitted into three peaks at 284.68, 286.15, and 288.68 ​eV (Fig. 3d), which were assigned to C–C, C–O, and carboxyl carbons (O–CO), respectively [19,32]. No shift was observed for the C 1s peaks of CQDs@In2S3-2 relative to In2S3, implying that CQDs are implanted or loaded on In-MIL-68.

The light-harvesting performance of obtained samples was evaluated by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 4 a). The In2S3 exhibited a strong light absorption in 300–800 ​nm with an absorption edge of 620 ​nm. Compared to In2S3, the optical absorption in 350–460 ​nm became strong after coupling of CQDs, but nearly unchanged in 500–800 ​nm. Based on the (αhν)2 versus hν and Mott–Schottky (MS) plots (Fig. S5), the band gap value (E g) of In2S3 was 2.24 ​eV with conductive band potential (E CB) of about −0.65 ​eV. Thus, the valence band potential (E VB) of In2S3 was 1.59 ​eV.

The photogenerated charge separation performance of prepared samples was analyzed using photoluminescence (PL) and photocurrent measurements. Compared to In2S3, the PL peak intensity of CQDs@In2S3-x decreased, indicating the suppressed electrons and holes recombination after coupling of CQDs (Fig. 4b) [33]. Moreover, the photocurrent of CQDs@In2S3-2 was higher than those of In2S3, CQDs@In2S3-1, and CQDs@In2S3-3 (Fig. 4c), indicating the efficient separation and longer lifetime of charge carriers of CQDs@In2S3-2. This is consistent with the PL results. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots were also measured to illustrate the electrical conductivity of the samples. As shown in Fig. 4d, CQDs@In2S3-2 had a smaller semicircle compared to other samples, suggesting coupling of appropriate amount of CQDs can largely accelerate interfacial electron transfer [34,35]. It can be seen that coupling of CQDs can promote the transfer and separation of electron-hole pairs of In2S3, thus more electrons may participate in Cr(VI) reduction reaction.

The catalytic activity of obtained samples was evaluated by reduction of Cr(VI) without of sacrificial agent. The Cr(VI) concentration was not obviously reduced in the presence of CQDs@In2S3-2 but under dark (Fig. 5 a), indicating that hollow structure not induce good adsorption of Cr(VI) on CQDs@In2S3-2, and light irradiation is necessary for Cr(VI) reduction. After irradiation of 90 ​min in solution with pH of 5.5, Cr(VI) was hardly reduced without catalyst, and In2S3 only reduced 75.9% Cr(VI). Moreover, pure CQDs could not reduce Cr(VI). After modification In2S3 with CQDs, all the obtained CQDs@In2S3-x exhibited enhanced Cr(VI) reductive efficiency under the same condition. Particularly, CQDs@In2S3-2 exhibited 99.4% reductive ratio within 90 ​min. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the DPC-Cr(VI) complex (Fig. 5b) confirmed that the absorption peak at 540 ​nm gradually decreased with the increase of irradiation time, and completely disappeared after 90 ​min. The Cr(VI) reductive ratio decreased when more CQDs was added (CQDs@In2S3-3), indicating that coupling of appropriate amount of CQDs can increase the charge separation of In2S3, and achieve an efficient Cr(VI) reduction. Furthermore, it was found that the Cr(VI) reductive ratio (96.7%) not decreased when the solution containing NaCl, CaCl2, or MgCl2, but largely decreased in the presence of Na2CO3 (Fig. 5c). This decrease could be attributed to the consumption of ⋅O2 − radicals by CO3 2− [36]. This further implies that ⋅O2 − radicals may participate in Cr(VI) reductive reaction. Meanwhile, acidic condition was beneficial for Cr(VI) reduction owing to the higher potential of Cr(VI) existed in forms of Cr2O7 2− or HCrO4 − (Fig. 5d). In solution with pH of 4, CQDs@In2S3-2 could reduce nearly 100% Cr(VI) within 45 ​min. When tap water was used to prepared Cr(VI) stock solution, CQDs@In2S3-2 still exhibited 99.0% Cr(VI) reductive ratio within 60 ​min in solution with pH of 4 (Fig. S6). These results indicate that the prepared CQDs@In2S3-2 catalyst has strong anti-interference capability.

The reactive rate constants (k) were obtained by fitting pseudo-first-order kinetic model to further compare the Cr(VI) reductive activity. As shown in Fig. 5e, the optimized CQDs@In2S3-2 exhibited the highest k value at natural pH, which was 4.3-fold larger than that of In2S3. As the solution pH was 4, the k value was 0.152 min−1 (Fig. S7), and surpassesed some documented k values reported in recent years (Table S2).

Considering the practical application of prepared samples, successive Cr(VI) reductive tests were performed to investigate the reusability of the CQDs@In2S3-2 sample. The Cr(VI) reductive ratio decreased by about 7% after four cyclic tests at unadjusted solution pH (Fig. 5f). As the initial solution pH was 4, the Cr(VI) reductive ratio decreased by about 10% (Fig. S8). Moreover, the XRD of the CQDs@In2S3-2 after Cr(VI) reductive tests showed the similar pattern with the fresh sample (Fig. S9). Tubular structure constructed by nanosheets was observed in the used CQDs@In2S3-2. These results indicate the acceptable reusability of CQDs@In2S3-2 in photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI).

To explore the mechanism of Cr(VI) reduction under CQDs@In2S3-2 catalysis, a series of quenching tests with scavengers were performed to determine the reactive species. Specifically, KBrO3, citric acid, TBA, and BQ were separately added into the system to capture electrons, holes, hydroxyl (•OH), and ·O2 − (Fig. 6 a) [3,37–39]. In the presence of TBA, the Cr(VI) reductive ratio slightly decreased owing to the competition for electrons between formation of •OH and reduction of Cr(VI). When BQ was added into the system, the Cr(VI) reductive ratio was dropped from 97.2% to 80.9% within 90 ​min, suggesting that ·O2 − is participate in the Cr(VI) reductive reaction. The Cr(VI) reductive ratio slightly increased in the presence of KBrO3 and citric acid, which may be resulted from the depletion of electrons and holes by KBrO3 and citric acid, respectively. Thus, the recombination of electrons and holes was suppressed, and more ·O2 − could be generated to reduce Cr(VI).

Based on the above results, the possible Cr(VI) reduction mechanism is proposed. Under visible-light illumination, the electrons are excited and migrate from the VB to the CB of In2S3 (Fig. 6b), which then transfer to the CQDs, resulting in efficient separation of electrons and holes. The hollow structure of CQDs@In2S3 might also suppress the recombination of charge carriers by reducing their migration distance. Because the E CB of In2S3 (−0.65 ​eV) is negative than the potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 ​eV vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)), the electrons gathered on CQDs can react with O2 to form ·O2 −, which then reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) (Eq. (2)). Additionally, the E VB of In2S3 (1.59 ​eV) is lower than that potential of •OH/H2O (2.27 ​eV vs. NHE), suggesting that the •OH can not produce by oxidation of H2O with holes [32]. In fact, the dissolved O2 can react with electrons to form ·O2 −, after which, H2O2 and •OH are generated through Eqs. (3) and (4). It was found that 4.57 ​μmol ​H2O2 was produced in 10 ​mg ​L−1 Cr(VI) solution after 90 ​min (Fig. S10). These processes accelerate the depletion of electrons. So in the presence of TBA, more •OH was quenched, more electrons was depleted. As a result, the generation of ·O2 − and Cr(VI) reductive reaction were suppressed. The possible Cr(VI) reductive routes are described as follows:
(1) e− ​+ ​O2 → ∙O2 −
(2) ·O2 − ​+ ​Cr(VI) → Cr(III) ​+ ​O2
(3) O2 − ​+ ​e− ​+ ​2H+ → H2O2
(4) H2O2 ​+ ​e− → OH− ​+ ​•OH
(5) 2H2O ​+ ​4h+ → O2 ​+ ​4H+

CQDs-modified hollow In2S3 tube with hierarchical nanosheets was successfully synthesized via sulfidation CQDs@In-MIL-68 for reduction of Cr(VI). About 100% Cr(VI) was reduced under CQDs@In2S3-2 catalysis within 45 ​min in solution with pH of 4 (catalyst dosage: 0.17 ​g ​L−1). Radicals trapping experiments demonstrated that ·O2 − was the main active specie to reduce Cr(VI). The enhanced Cr(VI) reductive capacity of the CQDs@In2S3 was ascribed to the CQDs modification and the enhanced charge carriers separation. This work provides some insights into the CQDs-modified semiconductors with enhanced photocatalytic pollutants removal performance.

Meng Chen: Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Chao Wang: Resources, Investigation. Lei Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jixiang Xu: Software, Data curation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52171140, 51772162, and 52072197), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021ME022), Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020ZD09).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssc.2022.123722.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Batch Cr(VI) removal by polyacrylamide-grafted sawdust: kinetics and thermodynamics
Water Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Raji, T.S. Anirudhan, Batch Cr(VI) removal by polyacrylamide-grafted sawdust: Kinetics and thermodynamics, Water Res. 32 (1998) 3772-3780.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in removal techniques of Cr(VI) toxic ion from aqueous solution: a comprehensive review
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Karimi-Maleh, A. Ayati, S. Ghanbari, Y. Orooji, B. Tanhaei, F. Karimi, M. Alizadeh, J. Rouhi, L. Fu, M. Sillanpaa, Recent advances in removal techniques of Cr(VI) toxic ion from aqueous solution: A comprehensive review, J. Mol. Liq. 329 (2021) 115062-115091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasma-assisted doping of nitrogen into cobalt sulfide for loading cadmium sulfide: a direct Z-scheme heterojunction for efficiently photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction under visible light
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.X. Zhang, L.P. Feng, P. Li, X. Chen, Y. Gao, Y.S. Gong, Z.L. Du, S. Zhang, A.C. Zhang, G.F. Chen, H. Wang, Plasma-assisted doping of nitrogen into cobalt sulfide for loading cadmium sulfide: A direct Z-scheme heterojunction for efficiently photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction under visible light, Chem. Eng. J. 417 (2021) 129222-129234.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of black hollow TiO2 nanotube-coated PDA@Ag2S heterostructures for efficient photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI)
J. Solid State Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Jiang, Z.D. Chen, P. Wang, P.Y. Gu, J.D. Liu, Z.Z. Zhang, Q.F. Xu, Preparation of black hollow TiO2 nanotube-coated PDA@Ag2S heterostructures for efficient photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), J. Solid State Chem. 307 (2022) 122865-122872.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: S-scheme carrier kinetic pathway constructed by covalent-anion channels in self-assembled cadmium/rhenium sulfide hybrids for efficient Cr (VI) reduction
J. Clean. Prod.
